<!-- 12 dimensions surveyed, every finding adversarially re-verified against source. -->
<!-- Raw findings: 73 | Confirmed: 71 | Uncertain: 0 | Refuted: 2 -->

# sentencesplit — Library Review

## Executive summary

sentencesplit is a well-engineered, pure-stdlib rule-based sentence segmenter with strong test discipline (949 passing tests, 96% line coverage, a regression suite keyed to real pySBD issue numbers) and a generally clean architecture organized around an explicit text/boundary phase pipeline and a cached Aho-Corasick abbreviation prefilter. The library is functionally healthy: the full suite is green and the happy path is solid across all 24 languages. The most important defects cluster around a few themes. First, **internal placeholder sentinels are ordinary printable Unicode** (`∯`, `♬`, `ȸ`, `&ᓴ&`, etc.); when these appear in real input the non-destructive contract breaks, with silent text loss under `clean=False` and visible corruption under `clean=True`. Second, several **correctness bugs in the rule engine** lose real boundaries: an index-misalignment in abbreviation scanning that flips split decisions, an over-eager orphan-merge heuristic, and a CJK quote/bracket gap that fails to split after `！`/`？`. Third, the codebase has a recurring **duplication/drift theme** — the CJK reporting-clause regex, the Latin `.)` resplit regex, and the CJK abbreviation rules are each copied across modules, and `en_es_zh` re-implements base logic that has already drifted. Fourth, **architecture documentation overstates completeness**: `LanguageProfile` is only a partial adapter and the Processor/Segmenter still reach into `self.lang.*` for many hooks, contradicting CLAUDE.md. Fifth, **data quality** in the large machine-generated abbreviation lists (Kazakh dotted entries, Dutch corruption, Italian/German duplicates) is unaudited and partly non-functional. The lone high-priority performance issue is O(N²) abbreviation scanning on long newline-free inputs. None of these block normal operation, but the sentinel collision, abbreviation misalignment, orphan-merge, and CJK `！？` gap are genuine correctness bugs worth fixing with regression tests.

## Top recommendations

1. **Move internal sentinels into the Private Use Area (or escape/track them on input).** Eliminates silent data loss and `clean=True` corruption when real input contains `∯`/`♬`/`ȸ`/`ȹ`/`&ᓴ&`-style characters. — `sentencesplit/lang/common/standard.py:319-341`, `sentencesplit/processor.py:140,263`, `sentencesplit/lang/common/common.py:10,19`
2. **Fix the abbreviation `findall` index misalignment with a single `finditer` pass.** The positional zip of two independent `findall` result sets assigns the case heuristic to the wrong occurrence and flips real split decisions in plain English and `en_legal`. — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:275-280`
3. **Extend `_CJK_QUOTE_RESPLIT_RE` to the full CJK terminal set.** CJK quotes/brackets ending in `！`/`？`/`!`/`?` (and ASCII `.`) followed by a new sentence currently never split — extremely common in CJK dialogue. — `sentencesplit/processor.py:17-19`
4. **Tighten `_merge_orphan_fragments` so it cannot swallow a full short sentence.** The `<=10`-char abbreviation-fragment branch wrongly merges digit-initial, stray-`)`, and short lowercase sentences. — `sentencesplit/processor.py:165-196`
5. **Fix the `EscapedHTMLTagRule` character class.** `[^gt;]` deletes ordinary prose between an escaped `<` and a bare `gt;`; a corrected pattern must constrain to a tag-name token, not "anything but `&`". — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:77`
6. **Reorder the Cleaner so escaped newlines are decoded first.** Escaped `\n` is materialized after newline collapse, so escaped vs real newline inputs segment differently — and the escaped form is the documented PDF/JSON use case. — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:104-107`
7. **Make `en_es_zh` apply the uppercase sentence-start heuristic to merged Spanish abbreviations.** Currently it under-splits common English words (`doc`/`dir`/`dom`) that both `en` and `es` split. — `sentencesplit/lang/en_es_zh.py:28,69`
8. **De-duplicate the CJK reporting-clause regex and the Latin `.)` resplit regex into single sources of truth.** Both are byte-identical copies already drifting risk; hoist into `lang/common/cjk.py` / import from `processor.py`. — `sentencesplit/lang/chinese.py:18-20`, `sentencesplit/lang/en_es_zh.py:26,29-31`, `sentencesplit/processor.py:20`
9. **Make Kazakh actually consult its abbreviation list, strip trailing periods, and widen the lowercase lookahead to Cyrillic.** ~50 dotted Kazakh abbreviations never suppress boundaries; the custom `replace()` override never reaches the list. — `sentencesplit/lang/kazakh.py:99-340`, `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:260`
10. **Enable `xfail_strict = true` and add a cross-language round-trip test.** Stops fixed bugs from rotting as silent xpasses; cheap insurance against script-specific span regressions across ~18 under-tested languages. — `pyproject.toml:87-88`, `tests/test_segmenter.py`
11. **Reconcile the `LanguageProfile` "single resolution point" claim with reality.** Either finish moving the ~12 raw `self.lang.*` hooks into the profile or soften the CLAUDE.md/docstring invariant. — `sentencesplit/processor.py` (multiple), `sentencesplit/language_profile.py`, `CLAUDE.md`
12. **Address the O(N²) abbreviation scan and fix the stale README publish step.** Replace per-occurrence full-text `re.sub` with one combined `re.sub` per abbreviation per line; correct README step 6 to state PyPI publishing is manual. — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:268-281`, `README.md:261`

---

## Correctness & bugs

**Internal sentinel characters in user input break the non-destructiveness contract** — *high* — `sentencesplit/lang/common/standard.py:319-341` (corroborated by the `api`, `processor`, and `abbrev` dimensions)
The processor uses ordinary printable Unicode codepoints as internal placeholders (`∯`→`.`, `♬`→`،`, `♭`→`:`, the `&ᓰ&`/`&ᓱ&`/`&ᓳ&`/`&ᓴ&`/`&ᓷ&`/`&ᓸ&` family, `&✂&`→`(`, `&⌬&`→`)`, the double-punct symbols `☉☇☈☄☊☋☌☍`, `ƪƪƪ`/`∮`/`☏` from the ellipsis rules, plus letter-class sentinels `ȸ`→`""` and `ȹ`→`"\n"`). `SubSymbolsRules`/`_sub_symbols_fast` unconditionally rewrites them on output. When any appear in real input, processed text diverges from the original, so `_match_spans` (segmenter.py:222-286) cannot match verbatim, falls back to a whitespace-flexible regex that over-matches, skips a real boundary, and drops trailing text. Verified: `Segmenter('en').segment("aJTF．MP9 2\t♬k！UtMXhk{Pr'S♬-b！b")` drops the final `！b` (`''.join(segments) != text`), breaking the explicitly-tested invariant (test_segmenter.py:285-313). Under `clean=True` the corruption is fully visible: `segment_clean('I love this song ♬ so much.')` → `['I love this song ، so much.']`; `'x ȸ y.'` → `'x y.'` (the `ȸ` letter vanishes entirely). *Fix:* move all sentinels into U+E000–U+F8FF (PUA), and/or escape any pre-existing sentinel occurrences in the input before processing and restore them after. Additionally, harden `_match_spans` to treat an unmatchable processed sentence as a divergence and preserve the corresponding raw original slice rather than skipping the boundary (this independently fixes the `clean=False` trailing-drop). Update `standard.py:22,339-340`, `processor.py:263`, `common.py:10,19` consistently. Add regression tests asserting `''.join(seg.segment(text)) == text` for inputs containing each sentinel, plus `clean=True` cases for `ȸ`/`ȹ`.

**Abbreviation `findall` index misalignment assigns the case heuristic to the wrong occurrence, flipping real split decisions** — *high* — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:275-280`
`search_for_abbreviations_in_string` computes `abbrev_match = match_re.findall(text)` (every occurrence) and `char_array = next_word_re.findall(text)` (only occurrences followed by `". X"`), then zips them by positional index. When an abbreviation appears both with and without a trailing period, the two lists differ in length, so the following-character (and the `upper` case heuristic in `scan_for_replacements`) is read from the wrong occurrence and applied globally to `txt`, flipping the final boundary. Verified in plain English — `Segmenter('en').segment('See Gov here. The Gov. The state acted.')` incorrectly joins — and in `en_legal`: `'The 9th Cir held. The 9th Cir. The panel reversed.'` keeps the second clause joined, whereas the isolated `'The 9th Cir. The panel reversed.'` splits correctly. *Fix:* replace the two positional `findall` lists with a single `finditer` pass that captures each occurrence's own following character (defaulting to empty). Add a regression test in `tests/regression/` with a period-less decoy occurrence *before* the real `ABBR.`, covering both plain `en` and `en_legal`.

**CJK quote/bracket ending in `！？!?` followed by a new sentence fails to split** — *high* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:17-19`
`_CJK_QUOTE_RESPLIT_RE`'s lookbehind only fires on a period-class terminal `[。．]` before a closing CJK quote/bracket. Punctuation inside `「」『』《》（）` is placeholdered by `CJKBetweenPunctuationMixin` so the main boundary regex never splits there, and this resplit pass is the only thing that can re-introduce the boundary. Because the lookbehind omits `！ ？ ! ?` (and the ASCII half-width `.`), any quote ending in an exclamation/question mark followed directly by a new CJK sentence is not split — extremely common in dialogue. Verified across zh and ja: `「快跑！」大家都散开了。` → 1 sentence (wrong); same for `『真的吗？』…`, `《摔跤吧！》…`, half-width `「OK!」…`, `「危ない！」みんな逃げた。`, `「本当に？」彼は驚いた。`, and `他说.」她笑了。`. The slanted-quote `“…”` path is unaffected, making the inconsistency between bracket types user-visible. *Fix:* extend the lookbehind to `[。．.！!?？]` (placeholder forms are unnecessary since `_sub_symbols_fast` restores real punctuation before resplit runs). The reporting-clause re-merge (`「先这样吧！」她回答。`) remains correct via `_merge_quote_continuations`. Add regression tests in `tests/regression/` before the fix for both the new split cases and the reporting-clause re-merge.

**Orphan-merge swallows legitimate short sentences with a non-uppercase start** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:165-196`
`_merge_orphan_fragments`'s third branch (lines 178-184) matches *any* trimmed segment `<=10` chars that ends with `.`, doesn't start uppercase, and contains an alphanumeric — far broader than the documented "short abbreviation fragment (e.g. `pp.`)". This swallows real sentences. Verified: `Processor('He walked away.) The end.').process()` → `['He walked away. ) The end.']` (boundary lost + spurious space), while the structurally identical `'He walked away.) The ending.'` (12-char next chunk) splits correctly; `seg.segment('I have two. 3 are red.')` does not split; `'Look here. go away.'` merges but `'Look here. go forward.'` splits. The decision flips purely on the arbitrary 10-char cutoff. *Fix:* require the fragment to be a single token (`' ' not in stripped[:-1]`) and exclude fragments whose first char is a closing bracket/paren `)]}`. Add regression tests for `'He walked away.) The end.'` and `'I have two. 3 are red.'` before the fix.

**`EscapedHTMLTagRule` character class is mistranslated** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:77`
`Rule(r"&lt;\/?[^gt;]*gt;", "")`: `[^gt;]` means "not g, t, or `;`", not "not `&gt;`", so it matches from any `&lt;` to the next literal `gt;`, deleting everything between even in non-HTML prose. Verified via the public API: `Segmenter('en', clean=True).segment('The value x &lt; 5 and y gt; 3 in the equation.')` → `['The value x  3 in the equation.']`. *Fix:* the naive `r"&lt;/?[^&]*&gt;"` is **also wrong** — `[^&]*` still spans `'< 5 and y >'` in `'x &lt; 5 and y &gt; 3'` and yields `'x  3'`. Constrain the inner content to a tag-name-like token, e.g. `r"&lt;/?\w+[^&]*?&gt;"`. Add a regression test in `tests/regression/` for escaped-angle-bracket math before fixing.

**Escaped newlines are materialized after the mid-sentence newline collapse** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:104-107`
`clean()` runs `remove_all_newlines()` and `replace_newlines()` (`\n`→`\r`) before `replace_escaped_newlines()` (`\\n`→ real `\n`), so an escaped backslash-n becomes a real newline only after every newline pass has run and is never collapsed. `Cleaner('It was a cold \nnight in the city.').clean()` (real newline) joins to one sentence, but the escaped-newline form leaves a stray literal newline — and the escaped form is the documented PDF/JSON use case. *Fix:* run `replace_escaped_newlines()` first, then the collapse/convert passes. Add a regression test asserting escaped and real newline inputs produce identical cleaned output.

**`HTMLTagRule` deletes valid `<...>` content (generics, math) under `clean=True`** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:74`
`HTMLTagRule` (`<\/?\w+...>`) is applied unconditionally in `clean()`, stripping any angle-bracket token starting with a word char. Verified: `Segmenter('en', clean=True).segment('Declare List<int> myList in code.')` → `['Declare List myList in code.']`. Doubly gated (opt-in `clean=True` + word-char-start span). *Fix (surgical):* gate `HTML.All` behind `doc_type == 'html'` so plain-text/code callers using `clean=True` aren't silently mutated; add a regression test capturing the chosen behavior.

**`TableOfContentsRule` over-matches ordinary prose containing 4+ periods then digits** — *low* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:39`
`Rule(r"\.{4,}\s*\d+-*\d*", "\r")` fires on non-TOC text: `'wait.... 42 things'` → `'wait\r things'`. Digits are required, so `'so.... really'` is safe but `'so....... 100 times'` is corrupted; it runs on all `clean=True` input. *Fix:* gate behind a `doc_type` indicating a TOC, or anchor to end-of-line / require a dot-leader context. Add a regression test in `tests/regression/` asserting ellipsis-in-prose is preserved.

**Placeholder sentinels in real input are rewritten on output in clean mode** — *low* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:140`, `sentencesplit/lang/common/standard.py:319-341` (corroborates the high sentinel finding from the `clean=True` angle)
Subset of the high-severity sentinel issue, visible specifically through `segment_clean()`/`clean=True` which return processed text directly: `'Value is ∯ and done.'` → `['Value is . and done.']`; `'Look ƪƪƪ at this thing.'` → `['Look ... at this thing.']`; `'Foo &ᓴ& bar baz qux.'` → `['Foo ! bar baz qux.']`. The reserved set includes `∯ ♬ ♭ &ᓰ& &ᓱ& &ᓳ& &ᓴ& &ᓷ& &ᓸ& &✂& &⌬& ☉☇☈☄☊☋☌☍ ♨ ☏ ƪƪƪ ∮ ȸ ȹ`. *Fix:* covered by the PUA/escape migration above; at minimum document the reserved codepoints.

**Sentinel set includes real Latin letters (`ȸ`, `ȹ`) that are silently deleted/newlined** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/common/standard.py:339-340`
`ȸ` (U+0238) maps to `""` (deleted) and `ȹ` (U+0239) maps to `"\n"`. Unlike symbol sentinels these are letters that can legitimately occur in text; loss occurs under `clean=True` (the default `clean=False`/`char_span` paths recover via `_match_spans`). The most concrete instance of the sentinel-collision problem — fix even if a full PUA migration is deferred.

**`en_es_zh` merges Spanish abbreviations into English text, causing false non-splits** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/lang/en_es_zh.py:28,69` (corroborated by `cjk` and `tests`)
The combined profile unions 141 Spanish-only abbreviations, including short common English words (`doc`, `dir`, `dom`, `cap`, `ap`, `cm`, `cv`). The overridden `scan_for_replacements` gates the uppercase sentence-start heuristic on `_ENGLISH_HEURISTIC_ABBREVIATIONS` (line 69, defined line 28), so Spanish-only abbreviations are unconditionally protected even before a capital. Verified: `'I read the doc. He left.'` splits under both `en` *and* `es` but not under `en_es_zh` — a genuine regression versus both component languages. *Fix:* extend the uppercase heuristic to the Spanish set too (or drop the `_ENGLISH_HEURISTIC_ABBREVIATIONS` gating so it applies to all merged abbreviations, matching the base class). Add a regression test in `tests/regression/`.

**`en_es_zh` merges a CJK closing quote followed by any lowercase Latin word** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/en_es_zh.py:136-148`
`_should_merge_quote_continuation` returns True whenever the previous segment ends in a quote-closer (`_QUOTE_CLOSER_RE` includes CJK `」』》】`) and the current segment starts lowercase. Verified: `结果是「好。」then nothing happened.` and `他说：「OK。」then he continued.` collapse to one sentence in `en_es_zh` but split in standalone `zh`. *Fix:* only apply the lowercase-continuation rule for Latin/ASCII quote closers; keep the CJK reporting-clause rule for CJK closers. Add a regression test.

**Roman-numeral list detection with periods is broken for multi-character numerals** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/lists_item_replacer.py:16,49,85-87`
`ALPHABETICAL_LIST_WITH_PERIODS` (line 16) and `ALPHABETICAL_LIST_LETTERS_AND_PERIODS_REGEX` (line 49) match only a single `[a-z]` before a period, so multi-char roman numerals (`ii`, `iii`, `iv`…) are never detected — the entire roman-with-periods path is dead. Verified: `re.findall(ALPHABETICAL_LIST_WITH_PERIODS, 'i. ii. iii.')` → `['i']`; `'Items i. first ii. second iii. third end.'` is not segmented as a list, while the parens variant `(i)/(ii)/(iii)` works. A dormant niche path inherited from pySBD; failure falls back to normal segmentation (no crash/corruption). *Fix:* give the roman path its own multi-char detection/replacement regexes — do **not** widen the shared line-49 regex to `[a-z]+`, as it is also used by the single-letter alphabetical path and would regress `a. b. c.` lists. Add a golden-rule test.

**Parenthetical single-letter references mis-detected as alphabetic lists** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/lists_item_replacer.py:19,216-227,229-239`
Consecutive parenthetical references like `'The value (a) was set. The value (b) was unset.'` are treated as an alphabetic list because `(a)`/`(b)` are sequential letters, fragmenting prose. There is **no distance guard** (the `<200` check exists only in the unrelated numbered-list `scan_lists`), so the trigger is pure consecutive-letter sequentiality at unlimited distance. *Fix:* require list-marker parens to be at a clause/line start (preceded by start-of-line, newline placeholder, or sentence-terminal punctuation) rather than any whitespace. The "followed by a capital" guard is unreliable (`'See note (a) Then read note (b) Today.'` still mis-splits). Add a regression test in `tests/regression/test_issues.py`.

**Single-quoted phrase at start of text is not protected** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/between_punctuation.py:14,16`
`BETWEEN_SINGLE_QUOTES_REGEX` and `BETWEEN_SINGLE_QUOTE_SLANTED_REGEX` both begin with `(?<=\s)`, so a single-quoted phrase opening the input is never matched. Verified: `"'stop now. go away.' he said"` splits into `["'stop now. go away.", "' he said"]` (dangling closer); the slanted variant behaves identically, whereas double quotes/parens and even slanted double quotes (`“…”`) protect at string start. Single quotes (straight and slanted) are the only outliers. *Fix:* allow a start-of-string anchor, mirroring the list-regex convention `(?:(?<=\s)|^|\A)`. Add a regression test in `tests/regression/`.

**Kazakh dotted abbreviations never suppress boundaries — list is largely dead weight** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/lang/kazakh.py:99-340` (corroborated by `lang_consistency` and `lang_big`)
~50 entries are stored with a trailing period (`авг.`, `обл.`, `сент.`…); `_AbbreviationData` strips only whitespace, so the key becomes `авг.` and `replace_period_of_abbr` appends another `\.`, matching only `авг..` which never occurs. Verified: `Segmenter('kk').segment('Ол 1990 авг. айында туды.')` splits mid-sentence after the abbreviation. The mechanism is worse than the simple data bug: Kazakh's `AbbreviationReplacer.replace()` (lines 331-340) fully overrides the base and **never invokes** `search_for_abbreviations_in_string`/`replace_period_of_abbr`, so the whole list (including dot-less entries like `англ`) is unconsulted; additionally the base lowercase lookahead is ASCII-only (`abbreviation_replacer.py:260`), so Cyrillic-after-abbreviation also fails. *Fix:* (a) make the Kazakh override call `super().replace()`/`search_for_abbreviations_in_string`, (b) strip trailing periods from the data, and (c) widen the lowercase lookahead to include Cyrillic (`а-яёА-Я` or a script-aware class). Add a regression test in `tests/regression/` for `авг.`/`обл.`/`англ.` before a lowercase Cyrillic word.

**Kazakh `MULTI_PERIOD_ABBREVIATION_REGEX` has a `b[a-z]` typo where `\b[a-z]` was intended** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/kazakh.py:15-17`
The Latin branch is `b[a-z](?:\.[a-z])+[.]` — a literal `b` — instead of word-boundary `\b[a-z]...`, while the Cyrillic branch correctly uses `\b`. Verified: matches `ba.b.`/`Bb.c.` but not `a.b.`, so generic Latin dotted initialisms are protected only when they start with `b`. *Fix:* change line 16 to `\b[a-z](?:\.[a-z])+[.]`; add a test asserting `a.b.` is protected.

**Hindi lists `.` as a Punctuation but omits it from `SENTENCE_BOUNDARY_REGEX`** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/hindi.py:10-11`
`Punctuations = ['।','|','.','!','?']` but the boundary char class lacks `.`. Verified: `Segmenter('hi').segment('First sentence. Second sentence.')` returns one segment, while Marathi (which has `.` in both lists) splits. *Fix:* prefer removing `.` from `Punctuations` (the danda `।` is the standard Hindi terminator; adding `.` risks over-splitting Latin abbreviations/decimals in mixed text) unless product demand exists. Add a regression test in `tests/regression/` for the chosen behavior.

**ExclamationWords alternation is leftmost-first, shadowing longer entries** — *low* — `sentencesplit/exclamation_words.py:15-22`
`!Kung` is listed before `!Kung-Ekoka`, so Python's leftmost-match alternation matches `!Kung` and leaves `-Ekoka` dangling. Currently benign (the `!` is still protected) but fragile. *Fix:* `sorted(EXCLAMATION_WORDS, key=len, reverse=True)`, matching the longest-first ordering already used for abbreviations.

**Exclamation-word entries lack word-boundary anchoring; brand names suppress genuine sentence-final boundaries** — *low* — `sentencesplit/exclamation_words.py:18-22`
No `\b`/lookaround, so listed tokens match as substrings (`'xYum!'` → `'xYum&ᓴ&'`), and `Yahoo!`/`Yum!`/`Y!J` protect their `!` even sentence-finally: `Segmenter('en').segment('I really love Yahoo! Do you?')` → one segment. *Fix:* anchor click-language entries with a trailing non-space requirement, and for brand names only protect `!` when *not* followed by whitespace + a sentence-start. Any change must not regress mid-sentence golden rules (test_english.py:108-109, test_english_clean.py:194 `"He works at Yahoo! and Y!J."`). Add a regression test covering `'Yahoo! Sentence.'` *and* asserting the existing merged cases still pass.

**Danish `"lb."` abbreviation entry is redundant** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/danish.py:252`
Stored with a trailing period, so its key becomes `lb.` and it can only match `lb..`. **Not** a correctness bug: bare `"lb"` already exists at line 251 and correctly protects the period (segmentation output is identical with or without line 252). *Fix:* simply delete line 252 (do not "change to `lb`", which would duplicate line 251).

**Deutsch/Italian abbreviation-list data defects** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/deutsch.py:60-61,93,96,115,161,164`, `sentencesplit/lang/italian.py:704,1661`
German entries carry stray/doubled whitespace (`'dr '`, `'ev '`, `'kath '`, `'str  '`, `'u.a  '`) salvaged only by `.strip()`, plus an `Ä`/`ä` case duplicate. Italian lists `'dir'` twice (671, 704) and `'psicol'` twice (1661, 1673) — both confirming unaudited machine generation, harmless at runtime via idempotent placeholder replacement. *Fix:* trim the German entries and drop one of `Ä`/`ä`; remove the duplicate `'dir'`@704 and `'psicol'`@1661; add `'it'` to `DEDUPED_ABBREVIATION_LANGUAGE_CODES` (tests/test_languages.py:12) and a case-insensitive `a.strip().lower()` dedup assertion across all languages.

**Dutch abbreviation list is a corrupted machine-generated dump** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/dutch.py:9-1597`
Split-compound fragments (`"aanw"`+`"vnw"`, `"adv"`+`"gen"`, `"b"`+`"en w"`), apostrophe-variant duplicates differing only by U+0027 vs U+2019 (lines 45-46, 777-778), French-legal entries (`amén`, `ann.dr.lg`) filed under iso `nl`, and empty `PREPOSITIVE_ABBREVIATIONS`/`NUMBER_ABBREVIATIONS` (lines 1596-1597). *Fix:* de-duplicate (normalize apostrophes, strip trailing periods), remove nonsense fragments, scope French entries or document that `nl` targets Belgian legal text, and populate `PREPOSITIVE_ABBREVIATIONS` with real Dutch titles.

---

## Architecture

**Processor bypasses `LanguageProfile` for ~12 language-specific hooks, contradicting the documented invariant** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:99-102,135,140,202,249,262,272,276,282`, `sentencesplit/language_profile.py`, `CLAUDE.md`
CLAUDE.md and the profile docstring claim the Processor reads "everything language-specific through `self.profile`", but it still reaches into `self.lang.*` for `Abbreviation.WithMultiplePeriodsAndEmailRule`, `GeoLocationRule`/`FileFormatRule`/`DotNetRule`, `SubSingleQuoteRule`, `SingleNewLineRule`+`EllipsisRules.All`, `SubSymbolsRules`, `ReinsertEllipsisRules.All`, `Punctuations`, `DoublePunctuationRules.All`, `QuestionMarkInQuotationRule`+`ExclamationPointRules.All`, and `Numbers.All`. Of these, only `Punctuations` (9 languages: am/ar/hy/my/el/hi/mr/fa/ur) and `Numbers` (deutsch.py, danish.py — *not* Slovak) are actually overridden by shipping languages; the rest are latent. Functionally correct — this is a doc-vs-implementation gap. *Fix:* either move the remaining hooks into `LanguageProfile.from_language()`, or soften the CLAUDE.md/docstring claim to describe the profile's actual (partial) coverage.

**`Cleaner`, `Processor`, and `Punctuations` are resolved outside `LanguageProfile`** — *low* — `sentencesplit/segmenter.py:81-82,102`, `sentencesplit/processor.py:49,249,262`
The Segmenter independently resolves `Cleaner`/`Processor` via `getattr` and reads `language_module.Punctuations`; the Processor also reads `self.lang.Punctuations` (lines 249,262) and constructs its own profile (line 49). Hook resolution is fragmented across two call sites with no single source of truth. *Fix:* add `cleaner_cls`, `processor_cls`, and `punctuations` to `LanguageProfile`, route both the Segmenter and the Processor's `Punctuations` reads through it.

**`CJK_REPORTING_CLAUSE_REGEX` hook bypasses `LanguageProfile` via `getattr`** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/common/cjk.py:67`
`CJKProcessor._merge_quote_continuations` uses `getattr(self.lang, "CJK_REPORTING_CLAUSE_REGEX", None)` (only zh/ja use this path; `en_es_zh` uses a module constant), asymmetric with `cjk_abbreviation_rules` which the profile already resolves. *Fix:* add `cjk_reporting_clause_re: re.Pattern[str] | None` to `LanguageProfile`, resolve with `ensure_compiled` in `from_language()`, read `self.profile.cjk_reporting_clause_re`.

**`from_language()` mixes graceful `getattr`-with-default and hard attribute access** — *low* — `sentencesplit/language_profile.py:33-48`
Optional hooks use `getattr` defaults, but the seven compiled-regex fields use bare attribute access, so a hand-rolled class missing them raises an opaque `AttributeError`. Note: the README does **not** document bare classes as supported (all examples inherit `Common, Standard`), so this is a cosmetic style nit, not a contract hazard. *Fix (low priority):* a single up-front validation guard, or `getattr(lang, NAME, Common.NAME)` defaults — be consistent.

**Global mutable registry (`LANGUAGE_CODES`) is the documented extension API with no thread-safety and process-wide side effects** — *low* — `sentencesplit/languages.py:67-170`, `README.md:194,240`
Custom registration mutates a module-global with no locking; registration is process-global and unscoped, and tests must poke the private `_removed` set to restore state (tests/test_languages.py:53-66). The `_load_language` race cannot double-import (importlib's lock returns the cached module), so the concrete hazard is limited to redundant cache writes (GIL-benign). *Fix:* provide an explicit `register_language`/`unregister` API, document the process-global non-thread-safe nature near the README examples, and consider a per-Segmenter registry override long-term.

**`LanguageProfile` rebuilt on every Processor construction; per-occurrence regex strings rebuilt** — see Performance section (same root, listed there to avoid duplication).

**Duplicated regexes/rule bodies across modules (drift hazard)** — *low/medium* — multiple (corroborated by `processor`, `cjk`, `lang_consistency`)
Several bodies are byte-identical copies already inviting drift:
- **CJK reporting-clause regex**: `Chinese.CJK_REPORTING_CLAUSE_REGEX` (chinese.py:18-20) == `en_es_zh._CJK_REPORTING_CLAUSE_RE` (en_es_zh.py:29-31). *Fix:* hoist a single shared regex into `lang/common/cjk.py`, referenced by both (this also unifies the divergent `_merge_quote_continuation` paths better than importing the Chinese attribute directly).
- **Latin `.)` resplit regex**: `processor._LATIN_RESPLIT_RE` (processor.py:20) == `en_es_zh._LATIN_PAREN_RESPLIT_RE` (en_es_zh.py:26). *Fix:* delete the local copy and import `_LATIN_RESPLIT_RE` (en_es_zh already imports several names from processor).
- **`CjkAbbreviationRules`** copied into chinese.py:34-38, japanese.py:55-59, en_es_zh.py:100-104 with divergent CJK ranges (`[一-鿿]` BMP-only in zh vs `[㐀-鿿]` including Extension-A in en_es_zh). Since en_es_zh subsumes Chinese, the narrower zh range is inconsistent. *Fix:* factor the shared ideograph char-class constant into `lang/common/cjk.py` (kana layered on for ja), choosing one canonical range.

**Arabic and Persian duplicate identical abbreviation/colon/comma logic** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/arabic.py:15-24`, `sentencesplit/lang/persian.py:16-24`
Byte-identical `ReplaceColonBetweenNumbersRule`, `ReplaceNonSentenceBoundaryCommaRule`, and `AbbreviationReplacer.scan_for_replacements` (pattern+replacement strings equal); the modules' `SENTENCE_BOUNDARY_REGEX`/`Punctuations` have already diverged (Arabic adds `،`, Persian doesn't). The shared `scan_for_replacements` uses `(?<={am})\.` with no whitespace boundary (a latent start-of-line edge-case weakness), now copied twice. Pure-refactor finding, all tests pass. *Fix:* extract a shared Arabic-script mixin (mirroring `CJKBetweenPunctuationMixin`) into `lang/common/`.

**Multi-thousand-line abbreviation lists inlined as Python source with no list-integrity tests** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/italian.py`, `sentencesplit/lang/dutch.py`, `sentencesplit/lang/danish.py`, `sentencesplit/lang/kazakh.py`
~3800 lines of inline string-literal data with no data-file infrastructure. A partial dedup test exists (`tests/test_languages.py` `test_language_abbreviations_do_not_repeat_literals`) but covers only `DEDUPED_ABBREVIATION_LANGUAGE_CODES = ('ar','da','es','fr','nl','sk')`, excluding `it`/`kk`. *Fix:* extend the dedup parametrization to all registered codes (flags Italian's duplicates) and add an assertion that every entry equals `entry.strip()` and does not end with `.` (flags the Kazakh/Dutch dotted entries). Moving Italian/Dutch to packaged data via `importlib.resources` is a reasonable secondary improvement.

---

## Cross-language consistency

(The Hindi boundary-regex asymmetry, Kazakh typo, Arabic/Persian duplication, Kazakh dotted-list dead weight, Deutsch/Italian/Dutch data defects, and the CJK reporting-clause / CjkAbbreviationRules duplication are the seam-level cross-language issues; most are detailed under Correctness & Architecture above to avoid duplication.)

**Empty `__init__` overrides that only call `super()` remain in three modules** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/danish.py:40-41`, `sentencesplit/lang/deutsch.py:31-32,217-218`, `sentencesplit/lang/kazakh.py:20-21`
Pure boilerplate that contradicts commit 78ae045's cleanup. *Fix:* delete the no-op `__init__` methods in `Danish.AbbreviationReplacer`, `Deutsch.Processor`, `Deutsch.AbbreviationReplacer`, and `Kazakh.Processor`.

**English-specific `for N. x` guard hardcoded in the generic list replacer** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lists_item_replacer.py:126-136`
`add_line_breaks_for_numbered_list_with_periods()` suppresses splits matching the literal English regex `r"for\s\d{1,2}♨\s[a-z]"` (the placeholder is `♨` U+2668, not `♧`), living in the shared base used by all 24 languages. Blast radius is narrow (the trailing `[a-z]` limits firing to Latin-script followers). *Fix:* move the locale phrase into an overridable class attribute (e.g. `NUMBERED_LIST_FALSE_POSITIVE_RE`).

---

## Gaps & missing coverage

**PDF mode performs no de-hyphenation** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:82-87,139-145`
`NewLineInMiddleOfSentenceNoSpacesRule` (`\n(?=[a-z])`→`" "`) turns a soft hyphenation break into a hyphen + space: `Segmenter('en', clean=True, doc_type='pdf').segment('This is a hyphen-\nated word in pdf.')` → `['This is a hyphen- ated word in pdf.']`. README only promises "PDF line-break artifact" handling, so the framing is that it silently omits the most common artifact (not that an explicit de-hyphenation promise is broken). Affects only opt-in `doc_type='pdf'` and degrades token quality, not sentence counts. *Fix:* add `Rule(r"(?<=[a-z])-\n(?=[a-z])", "")` before `NewLineInMiddleOfSentenceNoSpacesRule`, with a regression test for `'hyphen-\nated'`.

**Multi-letter alphabetic list markers `(aa)`, `(bb)`, `aa.` are never detected** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lists_item_replacer.py:13,16,49,234`
`LATIN_NUMERALS = list(string.ascii_lowercase)` is single-char, and the period-path regexes (lines 16, 49) match only a single `[a-z]`. Verified: `'Codes aa) one bb) two cc) three end.'` is not segmented. A complete fix must address both the period-path regexes (add `+`, already present for parens) and the base-26 alphabet/index model. *Fix or document* as a deliberate single-letter limitation.

**`NewLineFollowedByBulletRule` pattern `r"\n(?=•')"` is dead** — *low* — `sentencesplit/cleaner.py:63`
The lookahead requires a bullet immediately followed by an apostrophe — never occurs in real input. In practice PDF bullet lists are still split correctly (other rules handle it; the test at test_segmenter.py:434 passes even with this rule neutralized), so it is dead/ineffective code, not wrong output. *Fix:* drop the spurious apostrophe (`r"\n(?=•)"`) only if a real need is demonstrated, or remove the rule.

**Japanese `Cleaner.__init__` silently discards `doc_type`, so `pdf` mode is a no-op for ja** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lang/japanese.py:18-24`
Accepts `doc_type` but calls `super().__init__(text, lang)` without it; `clean()` fully overrides the base. The constructor permits `Segmenter('ja', clean=True, doc_type='pdf')`, so callers get a near-no-op silently. The override is intentional (particle-aware join, validated by tests). *Fix:* document that PDF cleaning is unsupported for ja, or forward `doc_type` and raise/log when it is `'pdf'`.

**`doc_type` accepts arbitrary values without validation** — *low* — `sentencesplit/segmenter.py:39`
Only `'pdf'` triggers handling; `'PDF'`/`'ocr'`/typos are silently ignored. `split_mode` and `language` both validate, so `doc_type` is the outlier. *Fix:* validate against `{None, 'pdf'}` in `__init__` and raise `ValueError`.

**`en_es_zh` lookahead probe uses Latin stems for CJK content** — *low* — `sentencesplit/segmenter.py:13-28`
`_LANGUAGE_LOOKAHEAD_STEMS` maps `zh`/`ja` but not `en_es_zh`, which falls back to the Latin default. Largely inert (CJK full stops return before probing), so the stem only matters for `.`/`．` boundaries. *Fix:* add an `en_es_zh` entry combining Latin + CJK stems, or document that stem selection only affects period boundaries.

---

## Performance

**Abbreviation replacement is O(N²) on long newline-free inputs** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:268-281,308-344,78-82,254-266`
`search_for_abbreviations_in_string()` runs per "line" (text between `\r` markers; the whole input when newline-free). For each present abbreviation it collects all occurrences and calls `scan_for_replacements()` once per occurrence, each of which runs a full-text `re.sub`. Work grows as O(occurrences × N) = quadratic in line length. Measured: 16× input → 256× scan work; ~4.5s for a 32KB single-line input; 9.8 → 205 µs/char. Newline-separated corpora are unaffected (flat ~9-10 µs/char) because `splitlines(True)` keeps each scan short. *Fix:* replace per-occurrence full-text `re.sub` with a single combined `re.sub` per abbreviation per line (the lookbehind-anchored pattern already matches all occurrences; only the per-occurrence `char` case-heuristic needs the positional split). Add a perf regression test asserting roughly linear scaling.

**`LanguageProfile.from_language()` and the AbbreviationReplacer rebuilt on every `segment()` call** — *low* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:49,284-285`, `sentencesplit/language_profile.py:31-49`
A new Processor (and profile) is built per call; the profile is fully determined by the immutable language class. ~10 µs/call (dominated by getattr + dataclass allocation, not regex compilation — `ensure_compiled` is a no-op `isinstance` check), ~2.5% of a short call, multiplied in the lookahead path (3-4 probes). *Fix:* `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)` on `from_language` keyed on the language class, or cache the profile on the Segmenter.

**Per-occurrence regex strings rebuilt and `re.escape` recomputed** — *low* — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:326,259-265,78-82,288-289`
`scan_for_replacements` recomputes `re.escape(am_stripped)` per occurrence; the number-abbr suffix patterns (288-289) are constant literals rebuilt each call. *Important correction:* do **not** substitute the precomputed `escaped` for `re.escape(am_stripped)` and do **not** precompile keyed only by `boundary_class+escaped` — the period-replacement regexes are case-sensitive and matched casing varies per occurrence (`'no'` vs `'No'`), so either change would break case-variant abbreviations like `No.`. Safe wins only: hoist the constant suffix literals to module-level names, and drop the unused `stripped`/`escaped` params for clarity. Net impact ~3%; the real hotspot is `re.Pattern.sub` (~70%), unaddressed by any of these.

**Per-segment placeholder restoration does 21 sequential `str.replace()` passes** — *low* — `sentencesplit/processor.py:37-41`, `sentencesplit/lang/common/standard.py:319-341`
`_sub_symbols_fast` runs 21 `str.replace()` per emitted segment. 13 entries have single-char sources (including `☉/☇/☈/☄/☊/☋/☌/☍`, which have multi-char targets that `str.maketrans` handles fine) and fold into one `str.translate()` pass; only the 8 multi-char-source placeholders (`&ᓰ&`/`&ᓱ&`/`&ᓳ&`/`&ᓴ&`/`&ᓷ&`/`&ᓸ&`, `&✂&`, `&⌬&`) still need `str.replace`. Modest micro-optimization. *Fix:* split into a `str.maketrans` table plus residual multi-char replacements, or accept as-is (low priority relative to the O(N²) issue).

**Shared class-level caches read-modify-written without locking** — *low* — `sentencesplit/abbreviation_replacer.py:152-154,202-213`
`_data_cache`/`_boundary_regex_cache` use unlocked check-then-set. Safe under CPython's GIL (worst case: redundant build, no corruption). *Correction:* CI uses standard GIL builds (3.11-3.14), **not** free-threaded `3.13t/3.14t`, so the race is hypothetical for this repo. *Fix (only if free-threading becomes a goal):* a module-level `threading.Lock`, or pre-populate at import.

---

## Tests

**`en_es_zh` diverges from base English on double-period input with no test guarding it** — *medium* — `tests/lang/test_en_es_zh.py`, `tests/regression/test_issues.py:176`
Regression #78 locks in that English segments `"Sentence. .. Next sentence."` into `["Sentence. ", ".. ", ...]`, but `en_es_zh` shatters the `".. "` fragment into two single-char segments. *Correction to root cause:* the divergence originates in `check_for_punctuation` (the CJK between-punctuation/boundary regexes from `CJKBoundaryProfile`), **not** the `en_es_zh` `_resplit_segments`/`_merge_orphan_fragments` overrides. *Fix:* add a regression test asserting `Segmenter('en_es_zh').segment('Sentence. .. Next sentence. Next next sentence.')` matches base English; when fixing, investigate the CJK boundary handling of bare consecutive ASCII periods. (Drop `'Hello. ... World.'` from the plan — it does not currently diverge.)

**CJK abbreviation-period overrides (chinese.py/japanese.py) are behavior-affecting but uncovered** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/lang/chinese.py:23-32`, `sentencesplit/lang/japanese.py:42-53`
zh/ja override `replace_period_of_abbr` to add a CJK-character alternative protecting an abbreviation's period before a CJK char; coverage shows these lines never execute. Verified live: `'He works at Inc.中文继续。'` stays joined; `'Mr.中文继续。'` splits. *Fix:* add golden cases exercising an inherited English abbreviation (`Inc.`, `adj.`, `co.`) immediately followed by a CJK char, plus a contrasting case that should still split.

**`xfail_strict` not enabled — fixed bugs leave stale xfails that silently xpass** — *medium* — `pyproject.toml:87-88`
9 xfail-marked cases (e.g. `Pt.` not in abbreviation list, regression #39 `Fig.`, #83 four-dot ellipsis). With no `xfail_strict=true` and no `addopts`, an xfail that starts passing is reported `xpassed` and does not fail the suite, so a future fix rots unnoticed. *Fix:* set `xfail_strict = true`; optionally add `addopts = "-ra"`.

**`_wait_with_full_probe` lookahead fallback is uncovered and the test comment claiming it unreachable is wrong** — *medium* — `sentencesplit/segmenter.py:154-168,220`, `tests/test_segmenter.py:316-321`
The test file admits the team could not construct a reaching input. The branch **is** reachable: `Segmenter('en', clean=True).should_wait_for_more("List:\n1. apple\n2. banana.")` executes it (rfind == -1 because list-item processing rewrites `\r` to spaces in the processed segment but `analysis_text` keeps `\r`); the sentinel-collision inputs (`'A ♬ B.'`, `'foo ∯ bar.'`) also reach it. *Fix:* add a `clean=True` list-item lookahead regression test pinning the fallback's behavior and replace the false "we think this is dead" comment with one describing the real trigger. Do **not** replace the branch with assert/raise — it is reachable.

**~18 languages tested only via the default path with whitespace-stripped assertions** — *low* — `tests/lang/test_{amharic,polish,burmese,arabic,russian,hindi,persian,armenian,bulgarian,greek,urdu,marathi,kazakh,tagalog,dutch,slovak,french,italian}.py`
Only en/zh/ja/en_es_zh assert round-trip non-destructiveness or exercise `char_span` (via `*_no_clean_with_span_fixture`); only danish/deutsch/spanish/japanese/english_clean exercise `clean=True`. Round-trip currently holds for sampled languages, so this is a coverage gap, not a live bug. *Fix:* add one parametrized test over `sorted(LANGUAGE_CODES)` asserting `''.join(seg.segment(sample)) == sample` and a `char_span` round-trip per script (mirroring `test_segment_with_lookahead_across_all_languages` at tests/test_segmenter.py:206).

**`test_cleaner.py` covers only happy-path English** — *low* — `tests/test_cleaner.py:6-42`
*Correction:* `doc_type='pdf'` is covered at the integration level (test_segmenter.py `PDF_TEST_DATA`/`test_en_pdf_type` lines 463-469, plus de/es/da) and the `&ᓷ&` placeholder in `test_punctuation_replacer.py`. Genuinely uncovered: `EscapedHTMLTagRule`/`HTMLTagRule` on non-tag angle brackets, `clean()` idempotency, escaped-vs-real-newline parity, and the Japanese Cleaner override. *Fix:* add those tests; land the failing math/hyphenation cases under `tests/regression/` before the cleaner fixes per CLAUDE.md.

**Defensive guards in `ListItemReplacer` are dead after the alphabet filter** — *low* — `sentencesplit/lists_item_replacer.py:206-222`
`iterate_alphabet_array` filters `list_array` to items in `alphabet` (line 234) before dispatch, so the `if a not in alphabet: return` re-checks (lines 208/210/219/221 → 209/211/220/222) can never fire; lines 207/218 (`not alphabet`) are dead because the alphabet constants are always non-empty. Not a correctness bug — it inflates the apparent coverage gap. *Fix:* remove the redundant guards (or assert the invariant); the only callers are inside `iterate_alphabet_array`.

---

## Docs & packaging

**README "Releasing" step 6 claims the Release workflow auto-triggers PyPI publish, but it does not** — *medium* — `README.md:261`
`release.yml` has a single `release` job (checkout, uv, git config, `semantic-release version`) with no step invoking `publish.yml`; `publish.yml` is triggered only by `workflow_dispatch` (no `workflow_call`), so it cannot be called by another workflow. The CHANGELOG v0.0.3 entry confirms the workflows were intentionally decoupled. Mitigated by step 7 / the section header already describing the manual flow; failure mode is "nothing published" (recoverable). *Fix:* rewrite step 6 to state publishing is manual (merge with step 7, or describe running `Publish to PyPI` via `workflow_dispatch` with the new tag) — or, if auto-publish is desired, add a final `gh workflow run publish.yml -f ref=<tag>` step in `release.yml` and give `publish.yml` a `workflow_call`/`workflow_run` trigger.

**`segment()` has no docstring and its return type silently depends on `char_span`** — *low* — `sentencesplit/segmenter.py:288-290`
The primary entry point lacks a docstring while every secondary helper has one; its `list[str] | list[TextSpan]` return is determined entirely by the constructor's `char_span` flag with no method-level documentation. *Fix:* add a docstring stating it returns `list[str]` by default and `list[TextSpan]` when constructed with `char_span=True`, pointing to `segment_spans()` for span output independent of the flag.

**CHANGELOG.md is missing the v0.0.4 release** — *low* — `CHANGELOG.md:1`
Package is at 0.0.4 (tag exists, 2026-05-10) but the latest entry is v0.0.3. The CHANGELOG is hand-maintained (no `[tool.semantic_release]` changelog config); v0.0.4 contains user-facing changes (`feat(api): export TextSpan from package root`, `feat(typing): add py.typed marker`, the spacy optional-dependency group, uv-build bump). Pure docs drift, no package impact. *Fix:* add a `# v0.0.4` section; optionally configure `python-semantic-release` to generate the changelog.

**AGENTS.md lists a non-existent `clean/` directory** — *low* — `AGENTS.md:11`
The "Project Structure" section lists `- clean/: normalization rules.`; no such directory exists (normalization lives in `cleaner.py`, already listed on line 6). Diverges from both reality and CLAUDE.md. *Fix:* remove the bullet (or rename to `cleaner.py`).

**CLAUDE.md and CONTRIBUTING.md claim the package uses "only the `re` module"** — *low* — `CLAUDE.md:7`, `CONTRIBUTING.md:50`
The package also imports `unicodedata`, `string`, `importlib`, `functools`, `dataclasses`, `collections`, `email.utils`, `importlib.metadata` — all stdlib, so the README's "zero dependencies" remains accurate, but "only the `re` module" is factually wrong. *Fix:* change to "only the Python standard library" in both files.

---

## What's working well

- **Test discipline is strong**: 949 passing tests + 9 honest xfails, 96% line coverage, a regression suite keyed to real pySBD issue numbers, and public-API tests for lookahead/split_mode/spans/clean/doc_type. Golden-rule depth is good for high-traffic languages (en, zh, ja, es, de, en_legal).
- **The abbreviation subsystem is well-engineered**: the Aho-Corasick automaton is correctly used only as a substring *prefilter* (a superset of regex matches, so it can never cause a false negative), per-class caches are bounded and collision-free, and case-insensitivity is consistent.
- **The Processor pipeline is well-organized** into explicit text- and boundary-processing phases with largely correct ordering; the placeholder scheme, abbreviation protection, and Latin/CJK resplit passes behave as intended on the happy path.
- **The CJK subsystem is well-structured** (shared `CJKBoundaryProfile`/`CJKProcessor`/`CJKBetweenPunctuationMixin`) with correct handling of full-width double punctuation, ideographic commas/semicolons (correctly not split), char-span round-trips, and the Japanese line-join particle guard.
- **`LanguageProfile` is a genuine architectural improvement** (frozen dataclass, clean compiled-regex resolution, good dedicated test coverage), even if its documented completeness is overstated.
- **Performance is well-tuned for realistic input**: module-level pre-compiled regexes, the cached per-language abbreviation prefilter, and deliberate use of `str.replace` over regex for placeholder restoration; the one quadratic case only bites pathological long single-line inputs.
- **The 24-language set is structurally healthy**: every registered ISO code has a matching test file, every module defines `iso_code`, the "24 languages" claim is accurate, and the `LATIN_UPPERCASE_RESPLIT` opt-out is correctly confined to CJK.
- **Pure-stdlib, zero third-party runtime dependencies** — a real strength for a segmentation library, and `_match_spans` plus the `char_span`+`clean` constructor guard protect span mapping in the default modes.

---

# Completeness critique

*(An independent critic reviewed the report above for coverage gaps, weak findings, missed issues, and prioritization.)*



## 1. Coverage gaps

- **ReDoS / catastrophic backtracking** was never assessed. The report covers O(N²) *algorithmic* cost but no dimension audited the regexes themselves for exponential backtracking. `cleaner.py:74` `HTMLTagRule` has nested quantifiers `((\s+\w+(\s*=\s*...)?)+\s*|\s*)` — a classic backtracking shape — applied to untrusted `clean=True` input. This deserves an explicit ReDoS pass; for a library that ingests arbitrary documents it is a real availability concern, not hypothetical.
- **Unicode normalization / combining marks / surrogate handling.** The sentinel finding touches encoding, but no one checked NFC/NFD behavior, grapheme clusters, or whether `char_span` offsets are correct when input contains combining marks or astral-plane (>BMP) characters. Span offsets are Python str code-point indices; emoji/astral chars are single code points in Python but the report never validates span fidelity there.
- **spacy_component.py got zero scrutiny.** It is a listed public surface (entry point in pyproject.toml) and the `docs` dimension owns examples, but no finding addresses the spaCy factory's correctness, version compatibility, or whether it round-trips spans. It is an untested public API.
- **API stability / SemVer at 0.x.** The report flags the CHANGELOG gap but never asks whether the return-type-depends-on-constructor-flag design (`segment()` → `list[str] | list[TextSpan]`) is a stability liability, nor whether the mutable `LANGUAGE_CODES` registry is part of the committed public contract.
- **`__init__.py` export surface** was nominally owned by `api` but no finding lists what is/isn't exported or whether `__all__` exists — relevant given the report recommends new public functions (`register_language`).

## 2. Weak or dubious findings

- **"Per-segment placeholder restoration does 21 sequential str.replace passes" (perf, low).** Marked low and even the report concedes it's a micro-opt, but it's weaker than presented: it's invoked per *segment*, and segments are short, so this is negligible against the `re.sub`-dominated hot path the report itself identifies. Borderline noise.
- **"ExclamationWords alternation is leftmost-first, shadowing longer entries" (low).** The report admits it is "currently benign" (the `!` is protected either way). This is a non-issue dressed as a finding — the dangling `-Ekoka` has no behavioral consequence. Fine to note, but it's the weakest correctness entry.
- **"Global mutable registry … no thread-safety" (low).** The report correctly walks back its own severity (importlib lock, GIL-benign), landing on "redundant cache writes." After the walk-back there is essentially no defect — this is more architecture-taste than a finding.
- **The HTML/`doc_type` finding framing is slightly off.** The report says "gate `HTML.All` behind `doc_type == 'html'`", but `doc_type` is never validated to accept `'html'` (only `None`/`'pdf'` are meaningful per segmenter.py:39). The fix as written would require *also* adding an `'html'` doc_type, which the report doesn't connect. The bug is real (verified: `List<int>` → `List`), but the proposed fix is underspecified.

Everything I spot-checked live (sentinel `clean=False` 2-char drop, abbrev misalignment, CJK `！` non-split, orphan-merge, `en_es_zh` doc, escaped-HTML prose deletion, `en_es_zh` quote continuation) reproduced exactly as reported — the *high/medium* findings are solid.

## 3. Missed high-value issues

- **`ALPHABETICAL_LIST_WITH_PARENS` (lists_item_replacer.py:19) matches `[a-z]+` with no length cap, so any parenthetical lowercase word is a list-marker candidate.** This is a stronger, more general root cause than the report's "single-letter `(a)/(b)`" framing — e.g. consecutive `(see)`/`(note)` parentheticals can trigger list logic. The report's parenthetical finding undersells the scope.
- **`EXTRACT_ALPHABETICAL_LIST_LETTERS_REGEX` (line 46) has an unescaped `\(` vs `(`-mismatch with `ALPHABETICAL_LIST_WITH_PARENS` (line 19).** Line 46 uses `\([a-z]+` (literal paren) while line 19's first alternative uses `(?<=\()[a-z]+`. These two regexes are supposed to find the same markers but use different anchoring — a latent extract/replace mismatch the dedup-themed review missed.
- **`_TRAILING_SENTENCE_CLOSERS` / `_PERIOD_END_PUNCTUATION` (segmenter.py:30-31) used in `_match_spans` are not cross-referenced against the CJK terminal set.** The report flags the CJK `！？` resplit gap in the processor but doesn't check whether the *segmenter's* span-matching closer set is similarly missing `！？` — likely a parallel gap in lookahead/span trimming for CJK.

## 4. Prioritization check

The Top-12 ordering is mostly defensible (sentinel #1 and abbrev-misalignment #2 are the two genuine silent-data-loss/wrong-output bugs and belong at top). Suggested adjustments:

- **Promote the ReDoS/HTMLTagRule audit into the top tier** — it's absent entirely and is an availability risk on the untrusted `clean=True` path, arguably above several cosmetic dedup items (#8).
- **#3 (CJK `！？`) and #4 (orphan-merge) are correctly high**, but **#5 (EscapedHTMLTagRule) and #6 (escaped-newline order) should rank above #4** — both are verified visible corruption/divergence on the documented `clean=True`/PDF-JSON path, whereas the orphan-merge requires the somewhat contrived `.)` shape.
- **#8 (de-dup regexes) and #10 (xfail_strict) are pure maintainability** and sit too high relative to the real wrong-output bug at #11's territory; #10's `xfail_strict` is cheap and could move up, but the de-dup work (#8) should drop below the data-correctness items (#9 Kazakh).
- **#12 bundles two unrelated things** (O(N²) perf + stale README). The O(N²) scan is the report's only *medium* perf issue and a DoS-adjacent concern; it should be its own higher-ranked item rather than tail-bundled with a docs nit.

---

## Appendix: review methodology & per-dimension stats

This report was produced by a multi-agent workflow: **12 review dimensions** were each
surveyed by a dedicated deep-reader agent, and **every one of the 73 raw findings was
independently re-verified** by an adversarial skeptic that re-read the cited source and tried to refute it.
**71 were confirmed**, 0 uncertain, **2 refuted** (dropped).

| Dimension | Raw findings | Confirmed |
|---|---|---|
| `api` | 6 | 6 |
| `processor` | 5 | 5 |
| `abbrev` | 6 | 6 |
| `between_lists` | 6 | 6 |
| `profile_registry` | 8 | 6 |
| `cjk` | 4 | 4 |
| `cleaner` | 8 | 8 |
| `lang_consistency` | 8 | 8 |
| `lang_big` | 8 | 8 |
| `tests` | 6 | 6 |
| `perf` | 4 | 4 |
| `docs` | 4 | 4 |
